hinge: For temporary cementation of a lithium disilicate crown with the intent to evaluate sensitivity and later remove it for permanent cementation, non-eugenol zinc oxide cement is appropriate as it provides adequate retention without interfering with the adhesive bond of resin cement planned later.

Glass ionomer cement sets via an acid-base reaction between polyacrylic acid and fluoroaluminosilicate glass powder. Composite materials set through polymerization; compomer and RMGI have both acid-base and polymerization mechanisms.

Between two adjacent teeth there are four embrasures: gingival, incisal/occlusal, facial, and lingual. If considering a single tooth, there are four embrasures per proximal contact area. The item text suggests they are counting embrasures per tooth pair.
Type I GIC is formulated as a luting cement with lower powder/liquid ratio for thin film thickness. Type II is restorative; Type III is for liners and bases.

Flasking of a cast for denture processing is done using dental stone (Type III) or die stone to withstand the pressure and heat of acrylic processing. Plaster is too weak and would fracture under packing pressure.

During pulp development, Type III collagen is the predominant fibrillar collagen expressed by odontoblasts and pulp fibroblasts. Type I collagen predominates in mature dentin; Type III is characteristic of developing and healing pulp and periodontal tissues.

Small amalgam restoration replacement in a posterior tooth is best done with composite resin for esthetics and conservative tooth preparation with adhesive bonding. Gold and ceramic are more invasive and require more tooth reduction.

The four major classes of dental materials are: metals, ceramics (porcelain), polymers, and composites. Composite is a distinct class that combines polymer matrix with filler particles, bridging the gap between pure polymers and ceramics.

A 15-year-old is a minor without full legal capacity. While the mother is the legal guardian, the child's autonomy and desire should be considered. Doing the child's wish (extraction) with informed consent from the parent is the most patient-centered and ethical approach when the child understands the consequences.

Zinc phosphate cement has an exothermic setting reaction. Slow mixing on a cool glass slab dissipates heat and extends working time, allowing the cement to reach proper luting consistency without premature setting.

When a broken tooth cannot directly retain a rubber dam clamp, the adjacent tooth should be clamped and the dam stretched to include the target tooth, ensuring proper isolation for endodontic treatment. Cotton roll isolation is insufficient for RCT.
Clamp #212 is a retracted, winged clamp specifically designed for Class V cervical restorations. It retracts gingival tissue and provides access to the cervical area without interference.

Pain when clenching 3 days after a Class I amalgam with normal TMJ is classic for a high point contact (premature occlusal contact). Overcontour affects gingival health; galvanic reaction requires dissimilar metals.
W8 (W8A) is the standard clamp for upper molars. W7 is for lower molars; W2 and W4 are for premolars. The correct upper molar clamp is W8.

Altering the powder/liquid ratio (using less water) can accelerate gypsum setting. Hot tap water also accelerates setting but can weaken the final stone by incomplete hydration. The safest method among options that accelerates without compromising quality is adjusting P/L ratio within manufacturer recommendations.

This item is poorly formed with garbled options. A mild OD amalgam on a premolar could be replaced by composite or an onlay depending on the defect size. The listed option 'only' is ambiguous and suggests conservative repair — perhaps just polishing or repair.

For a Class I preparation with deep caries where remaining dentin thickness is 0.5 mm, RMGI can serve as both base and liner in a single-material approach (sandwich technique), providing chemical adhesion, fluoride release, thermal insulation, and adequate strength as a dentin replacement.

Moisture contamination of alginate powder before mixing causes premature reaction of the calcium sulfate component, producing a grainy, rough texture in the mixed material and a grainy impression surface.

Carbide bur effectiveness and survival are evaluated by observing metal removal at high speed before tooth contact — checking for concentricity, vibration, and cutting efficiency. This is a materials/lab question rather than a clinical one.

With 2 mm remaining dentin thickness for an amalgam restoration, no liner or base is required as the remaining dentin provides adequate thermal insulation and the pulp is sufficiently protected. Amalgam liners are only needed when RDT is less than 0.5-1 mm.

After whitening, oxygen free radicals remain in tooth structure for up to 2 weeks, which can interfere with resin bonding. Class III restorations should be replaced after 2 weeks to allow residual oxygen to dissipate and ensure proper bond strength.

This item is poorly formed. Deep bite and abnormal salvation suggest a challenging clinical environment for bonding. Impression technique is critical for proper fit and margins, but the stem mentions these as factors to prevent failure — none of the options directly address the stated risk factors.

Self-threaded pins provide the highest retention in dentin among pin types because they engage dentin actively through thread mechanics. Cemented pins have the least retention; friction-lock pins have intermediate retention.

Chronic vomiting from gag reflux or eating disorders bathes teeth in gastric acid (HCl), producing chemical erosion — smooth, polished enamel loss without bacterial involvement, typically on palatal surfaces of maxillary teeth. Abrasion is mechanical wear; abfraction is from occlusal loading.

Gypsum fracture during flasking is most commonly caused by insufficient bulk of the gypsum material, leading to thin, weak areas that cannot withstand packing pressure. Exothermic reaction and setting expansion contribute but insufficient bulk is the primary mechanical cause.

Severe immediate pain during in-office bleaching with adequate isolation indicates the bleaching gel has contacted gingival tissue through an undetected dam defect, causing chemical burns. Vital tooth sensitivity typically develops after the procedure, not within 3 minutes.

GIC is preferred over composite in certain applications because of its sustained fluoride release/leaching, which provides caries-preventive properties. Composite has a coefficient of thermal expansion closer to tooth structure than GIC, not vice versa.

Severe generalized pain especially in cervical areas of teeth with attrition describes dentin hypersensitivity from exposed dentinal tubules due to enamel loss from attrition at the cervical region where dentin is exposed.

To differentiate upper right (#11) from upper left (#21) central incisors before implant placement: #11 has a more rounded distal incisal corner, while #21 has a more rounded mesial incisal corner. The distal slope is longer than the mesial slope.
When converting an amalgam preparation to an onlay, the proximal box walls must diverge occlusally (not parallel or converging) to provide a path of insertion and withdrawal for the cast restoration.
